original_abstract:
This study investigates the reactive partitioning of cis- and trans-β-IEPOX onto hydrated inorganic seed particles in the absence of added acids. While no organic aerosol (OA) formation occurred on dry ammonium sulfate (AS), both isomers exhibited prompt and efficient OA growth on AS seeds with liquid water contents ranging from 40% to 75% by mass. This OA formation is identified as a kinetically limited process, where growth persists as long as a gas-phase IEPOX reservoir is available. Notably, no significant differences in growth rates or chemical composition were observed between the cis and trans isomers.

High OA loadings were also achieved on hydrated AS seeds treated with a base (NaOH), suggesting that the pH dependence of IEPOX reactive uptake is relatively weak for ammonium-containing particles. In contrast, no residual OA was detected after drying when Na⁺ was substituted for NH₄⁺. Using NaCl particles at an ionic strength of ~9 M, the Henry’s Law partitioning coefficient for IEPOX was determined to be 3 × 10⁷ M atm⁻¹ (−50 / +100%). When NH₄⁺ was present but sulfate was replaced by chloride, only minimal OA was produced, highlighting the potential role of nucleophilic strength in these reactions. 

Chemical analysis via online aerosol mass spectrometry and offline filter extracts revealed a composition rich in oxygenated hydrocarbons, organosulfates, and amines. These findings align with field observations of weak correlations between IEPOX-derived OA and particle acidity or liquid water, suggesting that in many atmospheric environments, the system is limited by nucleophile availability rather than water content or catalytic activity.
humanized_abstract:
This study investigated the reactive partitioning of cis- and trans-β-IEPOX onto hydrated inorganic seed particles in the absence of any added acids. No organic aerosol (OA) was formed on dry ammonium sulfate (AS). However, both isomers reacted to form OA very quickly on wet AS seeds across liquid water mass fractions ranging from 40% to 75%. The formation of the observed OA was determined to be a kinetically controlled process with the growth continuing as long as the gas-phase IEPOX was available. There were no differences in the growth rates or the composition of the products between the cis and trans isomers.

High OA loadings were also obtained on hydrated AS seeds that were treated with base (NaOH). The pH dependence of the IEPOX reactive uptake appears relatively weak for ammonium containing particles. The OA was however completely volatilized upon drying the particle when Na+ is used instead of NH4+. The Henry's Law constant of IEPOX with NaCl particles, at an ionic strength of about 9 M, is 3 × 10⁷ M atm⁻¹ (−50 / +100%). A much weaker nucleophilicity of the sulfur atom in sulfate as compared to the chloride one in chloride replacing sulfate experiments, led to very low (less than 5%) OA formation.

Composition analysis from online aerosol mass spectrometry (AMS) and offline filter extracts show high concentrations of oxygenated hydrocarbons, organosulfates and amines. These results match expectations from field measurements of poor correlation between IEPOX-derived OA and acidity or liquid water in SOA, suggesting that many conditions limit the catalytic process to nucleophile availability rather than water or activity.

document.output:
This study investigated the reactive partitioning of cis- and trans-β-IEPOX onto hydrated inorganic seed particles in the absence of any added acids. No organic aerosol (OA) was formed on dry ammonium sulfate (AS). However, both isomers reacted to form OA very quickly on wet AS seeds across liquid water mass fractions ranging from 40% to 75%. High OA loadings were also obtained on hydrated AS seeds that were treated with base (NaOH). The pH dependence of the IEPOX reactive uptake appears relatively weak for ammonium containing particles. The OA was however completely volatilized upon drying the particle when Na+ is used instead of NH4+. The Henry's Law constant of IEPOX with NaCl particles, at an ionic strength of about 9 M, is 3 × 10⁷ M atm⁻¹ (−50 / +100%). Composition analysis from online aerosol mass spectrometry (AMS) and offline filter extracts show high concentrations of oxygenated hydrocarbons, organosulfates and amines. These results match expectations from field measurements of poor correlation between IEPOX-derived OA and acidity or liquid water in SOA, suggesting that many conditions limit the catalytic process to nucleophile availability rather than water or activity.
document.input:
This study investigates the reactive partitioning of cis- and trans-β-IEPOX onto hydrated inorganic seed particles in the absence of added acids. While no organic aerosol (OA) formation occurred on dry ammonium sulfate (AS), both isomers exhibited prompt and efficient OA growth on AS seeds with liquid water contents ranging from 40% to 75% by mass. Chemical analysis via online aerosol mass spectrometry and offline filter extracts revealed a composition rich in oxygenated hydrocarbons, organosulfates, and amines. These findings align with field observations of weak correlations between IEPOX-derived OA and particle acidity or liquid water, suggesting that in many atmospheric environments, the system is limited by nucleophile availability rather than water content or catalytic activity.
